description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Smear phishing: a new Android vulnerability

tech_stack_fingerprints: SMS messages received on an Android device

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can send SMS messages to an Android user and wants to impersonate a contact by controlling the Sender ID.
prerequisites: Attacker has access to an SMS gateway that allows custom Sender IDs.
The target user has a contact saved on their phone whose number is a permutation of a valid Sender ID with non-numeric characters (e.g., a leading 'O' instead of '0').
applicability_signals: SMS messages received with alphanumeric Sender IDs.
SMS gateways that accept arbitrary alphanumeric Sender IDs.
Knowledge of the target's contacts and their associated phone numbers.
skip_if: The target device is an iPhone, as it does not exhibit this vulnerability.
The SMS gateway rejects Sender IDs that are purely numeric or easily parsable as numeric.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify an SMS gateway that allows arbitrary alphanumeric Sender IDs (e.g., ClickSend, Twilio).
Step 2: Research common SMS Sender ID formats and potential variations that might be misinterpreted by Android.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an SMS message with a modified Sender ID that Android's SMS handling will misinterpret as a real phone number. Example: Use 'O7890123456' instead of '07890123456' to impersonate a contact with number +447890123456.
Step 2: Send the crafted SMS to the target Android user's phone number.
Step 3: Observe how the message appears on the target's Android device. If successful, it will appear as a new message within an existing conversation with the spoofed contact.
Step 4: The message content can then be used for phishing or other malicious purposes.
payloads_or_patterns: Sender ID: O<numeric_string> (where <numeric_string> is part of a known contact's phone number)
Example SMS Gateway API call: Send SMS to <TARGET_PHONE_NUMBER> from Sender ID 'O7890123456' with content 'Please click this link: <MALICIOUS_LINK>'
tools_recommended: SMS Gateway API (e.g., ClickSend, Twilio) for sending crafted SMS messages.
Burp Suite or similar for analyzing network traffic if interacting with web-based SMS gateways.
success_indicators: The received SMS message appears in an existing conversation thread with a known contact on the target Android device.
The sender details of the SMS show the spoofed contact's name and a phone number that closely matches the crafted Sender ID (e.g., +447890123456).
The target user interacts with the malicious content within the spoofed SMS.
failure_indicators: The SMS message appears as a new conversation with an arbitrary Sender ID (e.g., 'Twitter', 'O7890123456').
The SMS message details show that the sender is not a real phone number or contact.
The SMS gateway rejects the Sender ID entirely.
Suggestion: If the Sender ID is rejected or not misinterpreted, try different permutations or obfuscation techniques for the Sender ID.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can impersonate any contact saved on the victim's Android phone by sending SMS messages that are indistinguishable from legitimate messages within existing conversations. This can be used for highly convincing phishing attacks, leading to credential theft, malware installation, or social engineering scams, as demonstrated by the Twitter hack.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test SMS handling on Android devices for Sender ID misinterpretation, especially when alphanumeric Sender IDs are used. Try prepending or appending non-numeric characters to numeric strings to see if they are parsed as valid phone numbers, leading to conversation spoofing.

meta.title: Blind SQL Injection at fasteditor.hema.com

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an API endpoint accepts an 'orderby' parameter that is directly used in a SQL query without proper sanitization, and the application returns detailed SQL errors.
prerequisites: Access to an authenticated user session.
applicability_signals: Endpoint exists: /api/user/<userID>/products
Presence of 'orderby' parameter in the query string or POST data.
SQL errors returned in the response when a single quote (') is appended to the 'orderby' parameter.
skip_if: No SQL errors are returned when injecting a single quote into the 'orderby' parameter.
Input is properly sanitized before being included in the SQL query.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify API endpoints within the target application that handle user data or product listings.
Step 2: Analyze query string and POST parameters for keywords suggestive of SQL operations (e.g., 'orderby', 'filter', 'sort', 'id').
Step 3: Attempt to inject a single quote (') into identified parameters and observe HTTP responses for SQL error messages.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to https://fasteditor.hema.com/api/user/<userID>/products?offset=0&limit=8&orderby=id+DESC' to observe the default behavior.
Step 2: Modify the 'orderby' parameter by appending a single quote: GET https://fasteditor.hema.com/api/user/<userID>/products?offset=0&limit=8&orderby=id+DESC'
Step 3: If a SQL error is returned, save the request to a file.
Step 4: Use SQLMap with the saved request file (`-r request.txt`), specifying the vulnerable parameter (`-p orderby`), and perform extensive database version fingerprinting (`-f`).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests)
SQLMap (for automated SQL injection detection and exploitation)
success_indicators: Receiving detailed SQL error messages from the server.
SQLMap successfully identifies the 'orderby' parameter as vulnerable to Boolean-based blind, UNION query-based blind, and error-based SQL injection.
Successful extraction of database schema or data using SQLMap or manual queries.
failure_indicators: No SQL errors or generic error messages are returned after injecting a single quote.
Application returns a 400 Bad Request or similar error, indicating input validation is preventing the quote from reaching the SQL query.
SQLMap fails to identify any SQL injection vulnerabilities for the 'orderby' parameter.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can potentially extract sensitive data from the database, including user information, order details, and any other data accessible by the database user associated with the application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test parameters used in SQL ORDER BY clauses for injection vulnerabilities, as they are often less scrutinized than WHERE clause parameters. Look for detailed SQL error messages as an indicator of potential blind SQL injection.

meta.title: The Case of the Missing Cache Keys

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique applies when a web server, specifically NGINX, is configured to cache responses for POST requests but the cache key does not include the request body.
prerequisites: Target server uses NGINX with proxy_cache configured.
proxy_cache_methods includes POST.
proxy_cache_key does NOT include $request_body.
applicability_signals: NGINX proxy_cache configuration.
`proxy_cache_methods` directive includes `POST`.
`proxy_cache_key` directive does not explicitly include `$request_body` or a similar element that would vary cache entries based on the body content.
skip_if: WAF blocks POST requests with suspicious payloads.
The cache key explicitly includes request body elements.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target uses NGINX as a reverse proxy or web server.
Step 2: Investigate NGINX configuration, particularly `proxy_cache_methods` and `proxy_cache_key` directives if accessible or inferable from behavior. Look for `proxy_cache_methods` including POST.

Step 3: Send a POST request with a malicious payload in the body to a known endpoint. Then send a GET request to the same endpoint without a body. Observe if the GET request's response reflects the payload from the POST request.
attack_steps:
Step 1: Send a POST request to a target endpoint with a payload designed to trigger a vulnerable behavior (e.g., XSS) in the response body. Example: POST /target.php HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

fname=<script>alert(1)</script>

Step 2: Immediately after, send a GET request to the same endpoint without any body. Example: GET /target.php HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Step 3: Observe the response to the GET request. If the XSS payload from the POST request is reflected, the cache has been poisoned.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /<path> HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

<PARAM_NAME>=<script>alert('XSS')</script>

GET /<path> HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: To send sequential POST and GET requests and observe responses.
Custom script (Python, Go): To automate sending POST requests with varying payloads and subsequent GET requests to check for cache poisoning.
success_indicators: The response to the subsequent GET request contains the injected payload from the POST request (e.g., an executed JavaScript alert if the payload was XSS).
failure_indicators: The GET request returns a fresh response not containing the payload, or a different cached response (e.g., 404, generic error). This indicates the cache key likely included the body or the server is not caching POST requests.
A WAF blocks the POST request with the malicious payload.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to poison the cache with malicious content, which is then served to other users. This can lead to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) if the reflected content is executable by the browser, or other vulnerabilities depending on the nature of the injected content and its processing by the application.
playbooks.key_lesson: When POST requests are allowed to be cached, ensure the `proxy_cache_key` includes `$request_body` or a similar directive to prevent cache poisoning.

meta.title: Amazon AWS Bastion - Logger Bypass

tech_stack_fingerprints: SSH banners indicating AWS Linux Bastion

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the AWS bastion host is configured to use /etc/bashrc and PROMPT_COMMAND for command logging.
prerequisites: Attacker has SSH access to the bastion host with an authenticated user account.
applicability_signals: The existence of a PROMPT_COMMAND variable in /etc/bashrc that executes the 'logger' command.
The PROMPT_COMMAND variable is set as read-only.
skip_if: PROMPT_COMMAND is not configured or is not used for logging.
The system does not use /etc/bashrc for shell initialization.
WAF or other security controls prevent modification of PATH or execution of specific commands.

recon_steps: Step 1: SSH into the AWS bastion host with authenticated credentials.
Step 2: Inspect the contents of /etc/bashrc to confirm the presence and configuration of the PROMPT_COMMAND variable, specifically looking for commands related to 'logger'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Execute 'bash --posix' to attempt to trigger a parsing failure in PROMPT_COMMAND.
Step 2: If Step 1 fails or does not disable logging, execute 'unset PROMPT_COMMAND' (if not read-only) or 'bash --norc --noprofile' followed by 'unset PROMPT_COMMAND'.
Step 3: If direct unsetting fails, create a local 'logger' executable with 'echo "" > logger && chmod +x logger'.
Step 4: Add the current directory to the PATH with 'export PATH=$PWD:$PATH'.
Step 5: Execute a command that should not be logged, e.g., 'echo "bypass successful"'.
tools_recommended: ssh: for connecting to the bastion host.
bash: for executing shell commands.
success_indicators: Executing commands after the bypass attempts and verifying they do not appear in /var/log/bastion/bastion.log or Cloudwatch logs.
The command 'echo "bypass successful"' not appearing in the bastion log.
failure_indicators: Commands continue to be logged after attempting the bypass techniques (e.g., 'bash --posix' doesn't cause PROMPT_COMMAND to fail, or the local logger is not used).
If 'unset PROMPT_COMMAND' returns 'cannot unset: readonly variable' and 'bash --norc' still logs commands, further investigation into alternative bypasses or system configurations is needed.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to execute commands on the bastion host without them being logged, potentially leading to further system compromise, data exfiltration, or privilege escalation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify command logging mechanisms by attempting to bypass them, especially in shared or hardened environments like bastion hosts, by leveraging shell features or manipulating the execution environment.

meta.title: Vulnerability in new TouchID feature put iCloud accounts at risk of being breached

affected_components: Sign in with Apple authentication flow
tech_stack_fingerprints: sign in with apple button present on login/signup pages

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target application implements 'Sign in with Apple' and does not sufficiently validate the returned user identifiers or authentication tokens.
prerequisites: Attacker has access to an Apple ID that has previously used 'Sign in with Apple' with the target application.
applicability_signals: Target application offers 'Sign in with Apple' option.
Application redirects to Apple's authentication servers for Sign in with Apple.
skip_if: Target application enforces strict validation of Apple's OAuth token and user identifier.
Target application uses a robust session management system that cannot be bypassed by reusing or spoofing authentication tokens.

recon_steps: Identify target applications that support 'Sign in with Apple'.
Observe the authentication flow when clicking the 'Sign in with Apple' button and note the redirects and parameters exchanged with Apple's authentication servers.
attack_steps: Initiate the 'Sign in with Apple' flow from the target application.
Intercept the OAuth authorization code returned by Apple.

Instead of completing the OAuth flow normally, replay a previously captured authorization code or attempt to manipulate parameters to reuse an existing authentication token associated with a different Apple ID that has previously logged into the target application.
If successful, the target application will authenticate the attacker using the identity of another user who previously signed in with Apple.
payloads_or_patterns: Intercepted OAuth authorization code from a previous Sign in with Apple session.
Potentially manipulated OAuth parameters such as 'state' or 'nonce' to replay or reuse tokens.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: For intercepting and manipulating HTTP requests/responses during the OAuth flow.
Apple ID management: To manage attacker-controlled Apple IDs.
success_indicators: Attacker is logged into the target application as a different user without the target user's explicit consent.
Application displays user-specific data belonging to another user.
failure_indicators: Application returns an invalid authentication token error or redirects back to the login page with an error message.
Application enforces strict token validation and rejects the manipulated request.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of users who have previously authenticated with 'Sign in with Apple' on the vulnerable application, allowing the attacker to access the victim's data and perform actions on their behalf within the application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate OAuth tokens and user identifiers returned by third-party authentication providers on the server-side, and ensure that tokens are not reusable or can be easily associated with a different user account.

meta.title: Banning users Race condition

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a feature allows users to report or ban other users, and the rate limiting on reports is insufficient, allowing multiple reports from different sessions to be processed concurrently.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated account.
Target user account exists.
Reporting a user requires a minimum number of reports (e.g., 100) to trigger a ban.
applicability_signals: Endpoint for reporting users exists (e.g., POST /user/victim).
Rate limiting on report requests is present but can be bypassed.
The application allows reporting a user only once per account, but doesn't prevent multiple reports from different accounts.
skip_if: Reporting feature is disabled.
Rate limiting effectively prevents multiple reports within a short timeframe, even from different accounts.
The ban threshold is extremely high and unattainable with reasonable resources.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the API endpoint used for reporting users and observe the HTTP method and request parameters.
Step 2: Attempt to report a user multiple times from the same account to confirm rate limiting is in place.
Step 3: Verify that the application requires a significant number of reports (e.g., 100+) to ban a user.
Step 4: Confirm that reports must originate from different accounts to reach the ban threshold.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use Burp Suite Turbo Intruder (or a similar tool) to send a large number of report requests concurrently to the target user's profile.
Step 2: Configure Turbo Intruder to send 300 concurrent requests (or adjust based on initial observations of request volume).
Step 3: Monitor the responses. Expect to see many 200 OKAY responses indicating successful report submissions.
Step 4: Wait for a significant period (hours) for the server to process all the reports and ban the victim account.
payloads_or_patterns: HTTP POST request to /user/<victim_user_id> with appropriate headers (Authorization, Cookie, Content-Type, etc.) and JSON payload indicating a report.

Turbo Intruder script:
def queueRequests(target, wordlists):
  engine = RequestEngine(endpoint=target.endpoint, concurrentConnections=300, requestsPerConnection=100, pipeline=True)
  for i in range(300):
    engine.queue(target.req, target.baseInput, gate='race1')
  engine.openGate('race1')
  engine.complete(timeout=60)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Turbo Intruder: for sending a high volume of concurrent requests.
Burp Suite Proxy: to intercept and analyze requests.
success_indicators: The victim user's account is successfully banned.
Subsequent attempts to access the victim's profile result in an error or a 'banned' status.
failure_indicators: Requests are consistently blocked by rate limiting before reaching the ban threshold (suggests stronger rate limiting than anticipated).
The number of reports processed does not increase, even with high concurrency (suggests a bottleneck or ineffective request handling).
The victim account is not banned after a prolonged waiting period (suggests the ban threshold is higher or other conditions apply).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can force the banning of any user account by overwhelming the reporting system with a race condition, even if the reporting is intended to be rate-limited or restricted to unique reports.
playbooks.key_lesson: When implementing features like reporting or banning, ensure robust concurrency control and transaction management to prevent race conditions where multiple operations on the same resource can lead to unintended states.

meta.title: Multi-factor Auth Bypass with Password Reset Function

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application enforces MFA but allows password resets without re-authentication or verification of the old password.
prerequisites: Attacker has registered an account on the target application with MFA enabled.
Attacker knows or can guess the username of the target account.
Attacker can observe the format of the password reset token sent via email.
applicability_signals: Password reset token is sent via email in a predictable format (e.g., contains username and a hash).
The password reset page does not require the current password to reset it.
skip_if: The password reset token is a completely random, unguessable string with no discernible pattern.
The application requires the old password to initiate a password reset.
The password reset process immediately asks for an MFA code after changing the password.

recon_steps: Step 1: Initiate a password reset for a known user account. Observe the format of the password reset link sent to the user's email. Note any included tokens and their encoding (e.g., Base64).
Step 2: Decode the observed token and identify any predictable components (e.g., username, hash). Note any random or seemingly arbitrary parts of the token.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the hashing algorithm used for the password reset token (e.g., SHA-256) by comparing known password hashes with parts of the token.
Step 2: Craft a new password reset token using the target user's username and the SHA-256 hash of a desired new password.
Step 3: Visit the password reset page for the target application first.
Step 4: Immediately after visiting the password reset page, navigate to the crafted password reset link containing the attacker-generated token.
Step 5: If successful, the password will be changed without requiring the old password or current MFA code, allowing login with the new password.
payloads_or_patterns: Python script to generate SHA-256 hash of a password, e.g., `hashlib.sha256(b'new_password').hexdigest()`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests and responses during the password reset process.
Python with hashlib and base64 libraries: For generating password hashes and encoding tokens.
success_indicators: The target application allows changing the password without prompting for the old password or the current MFA code.
The attacker can successfully log in to the target account using the newly set password.
failure_indicators: The reset password link returns 'no such token exist' or a similar error, indicating the token generation is incorrect or a CSRF-like token is missing. Pivot: Investigate if a session token is required in the password reset request.
The application still prompts for the MFA code after the password reset, indicating the MFA bypass condition is not met. Pivot: Look for other vulnerabilities like account takeover via other means.

playbooks.impact: Complete account takeover of the target user, including bypass of multi-factor authentication, allowing the attacker to access sensitive data, perform financial transactions, or impersonate the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test password reset functionalities for predictable tokens and the absence of an old password verification step, as these can lead to MFA bypass and account takeover.

meta.title: CVE-2020-9854: "Unauthd" (three) logic bugs ftw!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable on macOS versions prior to 10.15.5 and exploits a logic flaw in the `authd` service related to code signing verification.
prerequisites: Target system is running macOS < 10.15.5
Attacker has local access to the target machine
applicability_signals: Observation of `authd` service logs or behavior, though direct signals are limited due to the nature of the LPE.
skip_if: Target system is macOS >= 10.15.5
System Integrity Protection (SIP) is not disabled (though the exploit chain aims to bypass SIP)

recon_steps: Step 1: Confirm target OS version. This exploit is confirmed to work on macOS < 10.15.5.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious Mach-O binary with specific, arbitrary entitlements. This binary bypasses the integrity check within `authd` because `SecCodeCheckValidity` is not called before `SecCodeCopySigningInformation`.
Step 2: Use the crafted binary to interact with `authd` in a way that exploits the logic bug, allowing for privilege escalation.
Step 3: The writeup details a chain of three logic bugs to achieve kernel-level privileges with SIP bypass, implying further steps beyond the initial `authd` interaction.
payloads_or_patterns: Crafted Mach-O binary with arbitrary entitlements.
tools_recommended: macOS development tools (e.g., Xcode) for binary crafting
Disassemblers/Decompilers (e.g., IDA Pro, Ghidra) for analyzing `authd` and Security.framework code
System command-line tools for process interaction
success_indicators: Achieving root privileges (`uid=0`)
Bypassing System Integrity Protection (SIP)
Kernel-level code execution
failure_indicators: Privilege escalation attempts fail to achieve root or kernel-level access. This might indicate a patched vulnerability or an incorrect binary crafting.
macOS version is >= 10.15.5, which should have patched the vulnerability.

playbooks.impact: Local privilege escalation from a standard user to root, and potentially kernel-level privileges, with a bypass of System Integrity Protection (SIP). This allows an attacker to gain full control over the affected macOS system.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing services that interact with signed code, verify that integrity checks (`SecCodeCheckValidity`) are performed *before* extracting signing information (`SecCodeCopySigningInformation`) to prevent the exploitation of arbitrary entitlements.

meta.title: CVE-2020-13379 Unauthenticated Full-Read SSRF in Grafana

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Grafana instance is accessible and running a vulnerable version (3.0.1 - 7.0.1).
prerequisites: Target Grafana instance is accessible over the network.
applicability_signals: The existence of the /avatar/:hash route in the Grafana API.
skip_if: Grafana version is outside the vulnerable range.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a Grafana instance by browsing common ports (e.g., 3000) or through external reconnaissance. Confirm it is Grafana by looking for characteristic UI elements or HTTP headers.
Step 2: Check the Grafana version if possible to ensure it falls within the vulnerable range (3.0.1 - 7.0.1). This might be visible in the UI or in HTTP headers like 'X-Grafana-Version'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL that exploits the URL Parameter Injection in the /avatar/:hash endpoint. The payload should include the gravatarSource URL with a specially crafted 'd' parameter to trigger redirects.
Step 2: Construct the payload to leverage the open redirect vulnerability in i0.wp.com using the format `google.com%3f%3b/1.bp.blogspot.com/`.

Step 3: Combine the Grafana /avatar/ route with the gravatar parameter injection and the i0.wp.com open redirect to point to an arbitrary host (e.g., AWS Metadata API or an attacker-controlled server).
Step 4: Send a GET request to the crafted URL on the target Grafana instance.
Step 5: Monitor for any response from the target host (e.g., SSRF callback to an attacker-controlled server or sensitive data returned in the Grafana response).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send the crafted URL, and to monitor for callbacks on an attacker-controlled server.
success_indicators: Receiving a callback on an attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) indicating the SSRF request reached the target.
If targeting AWS Metadata API, successful retrieval of IAM credentials or other sensitive metadata in the Grafana response.
Receiving unexpected content or status codes that indicate data leakage from the target host.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 404 or other error response from Grafana indicating the /avatar/ endpoint is not found or inaccessible.
Receiving a direct connection error or timeout, suggesting network issues or a WAF blocking the request.
The target host does not respond to the SSRF request, which could indicate incorrect payload construction or a firewall.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) allows an attacker to force the Grafana server to make requests to arbitrary internal or external resources. This can lead to the retrieval of sensitive information from internal networks, such as cloud metadata APIs (e.g., AWS EC2 instance credentials), or interaction with other internal services. In some cases, it may also lead to further exploitation or pivot points into the internal infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering URL parameters that are URL-decoded and passed to downstream HTTP requests, investigate for parameter injection vulnerabilities that can be chained with redirect mechanisms to achieve SSRF.

meta.title: CVE-2020–9854: "Unauthd" - (three) logic bugs ftw!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has a user-level shell on a vulnerable macOS system and needs to escalate privileges to root, potentially bypassing System Integrity Protection (SIP).
prerequisites: Attacker has unprivileged user access on the target macOS system.
The target macOS version is vulnerable (prior to 10.15.5).
applicability_signals: Presence of the authd(8) service.
The Security.framework is in use for privileged operations.
skip_if: Target macOS version is patched (10.15.5 or later).
System Integrity Protection (SIP) is disabled, which might alter the exploitability of certain components.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the macOS version running on the target system.
Step 2: Confirm that the authd(8) service is running (e.g., using `ps aux | grep authd`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a custom binary or process that can be signed with arbitrary entitlements.
Step 2: Sign this crafted binary with specific entitlements that the authd(8) process unexpectedly trusts.
Step 3: Execute this crafted binary in a way that interacts with authd(8) and leverages the logic flaw in `SecCodeCopySigningInformation`.
Step 4: Chain this vulnerability with other logic bugs to achieve local privilege escalation to root, potentially bypassing SIP.
tools_recommended: codesign (for signing binaries with custom entitlements)
Mach-O binary analysis tools (e.g., otool, class-dump) for understanding Apple's frameworks
Custom exploit development environment
success_indicators: The attacker's process gains elevated privileges (e.g., becomes root).
System Integrity Protection (SIP) is bypassed.
The exploit chain successfully completes all stages leading to kernel-level execution (as described in the writeup).
failure_indicators: Authd(8) rejects the crafted binary due to valid code signing checks (if patched).
Entitlements are ignored or invalid.
The exploit chain stalls at any of its intermediate steps.

playbooks.impact: Local privilege escalation from a standard user to root on macOS, including the ability to bypass System Integrity Protection (SIP) and potentially gain kernel-level access.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify code integrity using `SecCodeCheckValidity` before processing code signing information, as relying solely on `SecCodeCopySigningInformation` can lead to the acceptance of arbitrarily signed code with unintended entitlements.

meta.title: One Click to Compromise -- Fun With ClickOnce Deployment Manifests

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when targeting environments that utilize or are susceptible to Microsoft ClickOnce deployments and where users may interact with `.application` files via web links or email attachments. It is particularly effective if the target uses Internet Explorer as their default browser.
prerequisites: Attacker can host a malicious `.application` file on a web server or send it as an email attachment.
Target user has ClickOnce runtime installed.
Target user can be tricked into opening the `.application` file.
Target user's browser is configured to automatically execute `.application` files (common with IE).
applicability_signals: Presence of `.application` file extensions in observed traffic or provided by the target.
User interactions that involve downloading or opening application installers.
Evidence of ClickOnce deployments within the target environment.
skip_if: Target user explicitly blocks or questions execution of unknown application installers.
Target environment has strict controls preventing ClickOnce execution.
WAF or email gateway blocks `.application` files.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target environment uses or is susceptible to ClickOnce deployments by observing `.application` file extensions or searching for relevant documentation.
Step 2: Determine if users interact with these files via email attachments or direct web links.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious ClickOnce deployment manifest (`.application` file). This manifest will point to a malicious application manifest (`.manifest` file).

Step 2: Host the malicious `.application` file and the associated `.manifest` file on a web server controlled by the attacker. The `.manifest` file should specify the execution of a malicious .NET assembly.
Step 3: Deliver the `.application` file to the victim via a link (e.g., in an email) or as an attachment.
Step 4: If the victim opens the `.application` file (especially via IE), the ClickOnce runtime (dfsvc.exe) will be triggered.
Step 5: The ClickOnce runtime will download and parse the `.application` file, which in turn requests the `.manifest` file.

Step 6: The `.manifest` file contains instructions for downloading and executing a .NET assembly specified by the attacker. This assembly can be designed to perform actions like credential harvesting (e.g., capturing NTLMv2 hashes) or establishing a reverse shell.
payloads_or_patterns: A template for a ClickOnce deployment manifest (.application) and application manifest (.manifest) pointing to a malicious .NET assembly. The attacker would replace placeholders for the assembly URL and assembly details.

Example:
`Deployment Manifest (.application):`
```xml ```
`Application Manifest (.manifest):`
```xml ```
The attacker would host `MaliciousApp.application`, `MaliciousApp.manifest`, and `MaliciousAssembly.dll` (or an executable wrapper) on their server. When `MaliciousAssembly.dll` is executed, it can be crafted to exfiltrate NTLM hashes or establish a shell.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To capture and analyze network traffic related to ClickOnce downloads and to host the malicious files.
PowerShell: To create custom .NET assemblies for payload execution.
Visual Studio: For developing and compiling .NET assemblies.
success_indicators: Receipt of NTLMv2 hashes over HTTP from the attacker's listening server.
Establishment of a reverse shell connection from the target machine to the attacker's command and control server.
Observed execution of the malicious .NET assembly on the target system.
failure_indicators: Target user receives security warnings and cancels the installation; no NTLM hashes or shell is obtained. (Pivot: Attempt other social engineering vectors or different payload types).
Antivirus software detects and blocks the malicious .NET assembly; no execution occurs. (Pivot: Try obfuscation techniques or different payload delivery methods).
ClickOnce runtime errors or network connectivity issues prevent manifest or assembly download; no execution. (Pivot: Verify file hosting and network paths).

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution on the target system, allowing for actions such as data exfiltration, credential harvesting (NTLMv2 hash disclosure), further lateral movement, and establishing persistent access.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate application deployment technologies like ClickOnce for potential attack vectors, as they often bypass standard security controls and can lead to one-click execution of arbitrary code.

meta.title: Zoom Security Exploit – Cracking Private Meeting Passwords
meta.vuln_type: CSRF | Weak Password Policy | Lack of Rate Limiting

tech_stack_fingerprints: Specific XHR requests during meeting join flow

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Zoom meeting is discoverable with its Meeting ID, and the meeting uses the default 6-digit numeric password protection.
prerequisites: Attacker knows the Meeting ID.
Meeting is password-protected with a 6-digit numeric password.
applicability_signals: Meeting join URL contains a meeting ID.
Zoom web client displays a password input field for a meeting.
The web client endpoint for verifying a password returns a 200 OK status or allows progression upon correct password submission.
skip_if: Meetings are not password protected.
Meetings use non-numeric or longer passwords.
Zoom implements rate limiting on password verification attempts.
Zoom requires user login to join meetings via the web client.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a Zoom meeting with a known Meeting ID. This can be obtained from public sources or shared links.
Step 2: Attempt to join the meeting via the Zoom web client (`https://zoom.us/wc/join/<MEETING_ID>`) to confirm if it is password protected and if a 6-digit numeric password is used.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to `https://zoom.us/wc/join/<MEETING_ID>` to access the meeting join page.
Step 2: Submit a 6-digit numeric password to the meeting join form. This can be done programmatically via automated requests.
Step 3: Observe the response. If the submitted password is correct, the user will be redirected to a privacy policy page or similar confirmation page.
Step 4: If the password is incorrect, the user will be presented with an error or prevented from proceeding. This allows for brute-forcing.
Step 5: Iterate through all possible 6-digit numeric passwords (000000 to 999999) until a correct password is found.
payloads_or_patterns: Example request structure (conceptual, requires specific request/response handling): POST to `/wc/join/<MEETING_ID>` with body `password=<6_DIGIT_PASSWORD>`
tools_recommended: Custom Python script with requests library: to automate the iteration and submission of passwords.
Burp Suite (Repeater/Intruder): to manually test or configure brute-force attacks against the join endpoint.
success_indicators: Successful redirection to a post-login page (e.g., privacy policy) after submitting a guessed password.
The response indicates successful authentication or allows further interaction with the meeting without an explicit 'incorrect password' message.
failure_indicators: Consistent 'incorrect password' errors for all attempts.
Rate limiting detected (e.g., IP ban, CAPTCHA, or HTTP 429 Too Many Requests).
Meeting ID is invalid or meeting does not exist.
Meeting uses non-numeric or longer password format.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can gain unauthorized access to private, password-protected Zoom meetings, potentially eavesdropping on conversations or accessing shared content.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement robust rate limiting on authentication endpoints, especially for password verification, and enforce strong password policies (e.g., minimum length, character complexity) to prevent brute-force attacks.

meta.title: Authorization bypass in Google’s ticketing system (Google-GUTS)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a ticketing system allows ticket updates via email and ticket IDs are predictable or discoverable.
prerequisites: An email address that can receive emails from Google GUTS.
Knowledge of the email address used to create a ticket.
applicability_signals: Ticket creation confirmation emails received.
Ticket IDs are sequential numbers.
Ticket updates are handled via email replies.
Emails are sent to a common processing address ('ext-ticket@google.com').
skip_if: Ticket IDs are complex, non-sequential, or cryptographically signed.
Ticket updates require API calls with authentication tokens.
Email replies to tickets are ignored or rejected.

recon_steps: Step 1: Initiate the supplier account setup process to obtain a ticket creation confirmation email from 'ext-ticket@google.com'.
Step 2: Observe the format of the ticket ID in the email subject line (e.g., 't/XXXXXXXX').

Step 3: Attempt to guess sequential ticket IDs to identify other existing tickets. Send emails with arbitrary ticket IDs to 'ext-ticket@google.com' to check for valid responses or error messages indicating ticket existence or non-existence.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a target ticket's ID (e.g., 't/12345678').
Step 2: Craft an email to 'ext-ticket@google.com' with the subject line formatted as 't/12345678' and include a malicious payload in the email body.
Step 3: Send the crafted email and await a confirmation email from 'ext-ticket@google.com' indicating the ticket has been updated.
payloads_or_patterns:
Subject: t/<TARGET_TICKET_ID>

Body: Please update the bank account details to <ATTACKER_BANK_DETAILS>. Also, confirm the current bounty payout amount and send it to attacker@example.com.
tools_recommended: Python script with smtplib and email libraries for sending emails.
Burp Suite or custom scripts for enumerating sequential ticket IDs.
success_indicators: Receiving an email from 'ext-ticket@google.com' confirming the ticket update with the attacker's injected content.
Observation of the altered ticket content through other means (if possible, e.g., if the ticket is publicly visible).
failure_indicators: Receiving an error message indicating an invalid ticket ID or that the email address is not authorized to update the ticket. Pivot: If IDs are sequential but not guessable, attempt to find a parameter that directly exposes ticket IDs or user IDs associated with tickets.
No confirmation email received, or the ticket remains unchanged. Pivot: Investigate other email addresses or communication channels used in the supplier onboarding process.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can update any ticket in the Google-GUTS system, potentially modifying sensitive supplier data such as bank account details, personal information, or bounty payout preferences, leading to financial fraud or data compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that email-based ticket update mechanisms enforce proper authorization checks, especially when ticket identifiers are predictable or easily enumerable.

meta.title: Authentication Token Leads To IDOR

tech_stack_fingerprints: Application responds with 200 OK to requests other than 'PATCH' without an auth token.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an event registration functionality allows users to upload resumes and provide names, and the application incorrectly handles authentication tokens for requests related to event data.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated account on the target website.
Target website implements an authorization token system.
applicability_signals: The application responds with a 200 OK status code to requests (other than 'PATCH') made without an authentication token.
Event registration functionality allows modification of user-submitted data (like name, email, resume).
skip_if: All requests, regardless of method, return 401 Unauthorized when an authentication token is missing.
Event registration data cannot be modified or accessed by other users.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to the application with two different user accounts.
Step 2: Navigate to the event registration feature. Observe the requests sent by the browser, particularly when registering for an event or modifying event details, using a proxy like Burp Suite.
Step 3: Analyze the request/response pairs for the event registration functionality. Identify parameters related to user identity, name, and resume uploads.
attack_steps: Step 1: Using the first authenticated account, register for an event. Note the parameters sent in the request (e.g., user ID, name, resume file).
Step 2: Log out of the first account and log in with the second account.
Step 3: Using the second account, attempt to register for the same event. Intercept this request with Burp Suite.

Step 4: Modify the request from the second account to use the identifier (e.g., user ID, or any other identifying parameter) and potentially resume file from the first account. Crucially, attempt to remove the authentication token from this modified request.
Step 5: Submit the modified request and observe the response.
payloads_or_patterns: Modify `PATCH` requests to `POST` or `GET` if they are related to user data submission/modification and are vulnerable to missing tokens.
Replace parameters like `email_id`, `name`, `id_no` with values from the attacker's first account. Remove `Authorization` header or token cookie if present.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, analyze, and modify HTTP requests.
success_indicators: The event registration is successful using the second account, but the resume and information submitted belong to the first account. This indicates an IDOR vulnerability where one user's event registration data can be overwritten or claimed by another user.
Response status code is 200 OK with no errors, and the event registration is associated with the attacker's second account but contains data from the first account.
failure_indicators: Response 401 Unauthorized: The application correctly requires an authentication token for this operation. Pivot to other endpoints or test other methods for the same endpoint.
Response 403 Forbidden: The application is checking authorization but might have other vulnerabilities. Analyze for broken access control.

Response 200 OK but no change in registration data: The IDOR might be specific to certain parameters or fields, or the vulnerability might not exist for this specific functionality. Continue testing other parameters or functionalities.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can register for events using another user's credentials or data, potentially leading to unauthorized access to event slots, submission of malicious resumes, or impersonation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test APIs and functionalities that handle user-submitted data for IDOR vulnerabilities by attempting to modify or submit data using one user's identifiers while authenticated as another user, especially when authentication tokens are expected but not enforced.

meta.title: CSRF + Open Redirect To Account Takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the application allows adding a secondary email address and lacks proper CSRF protection for this functionality, especially if previously added email addresses can be re-added without full validation.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an account on the target application.
Attacker must know the email address of the victim.
applicability_signals: Presence of an 'Add Secondary Email' feature.
Interceptable requests for adding a secondary email.
Absence or ineffective validation of anti-CSRF tokens for the secondary email addition request.
Confirmation link sent to the secondary email address upon addition.
skip_if: Strict CSRF token validation on all sensitive requests.
The 'Add Secondary Email' functionality is not present or is adequately protected.
The application does not send confirmation links for secondary email additions.

recon_steps: Step 1: Create an account on the target application.
Step 2: Navigate to the account settings or profile page.
Step 3: Locate the 'Add Secondary Email' functionality.
Step 4: Add a known email address (e.g., attacker's own secondary email) to their account, intercepting the request with Burp Suite.
Step 5: Analyze the intercepted request for anti-CSRF tokens.
Step 6: Remove the anti-CSRF token value from the request and re-send it to observe the server's response and check if a confirmation email is still sent.
attack_steps: Step 1: Add the attacker's chosen email address (e.g., attacker@gmail.com) to the attacker's own account as a secondary email, confirming it through the confirmation link.
Step 2: Remove this same email address from the attacker's account.

Step 3: Craft a CSRF exploit (e.g., HTML form) targeting the 'Add Secondary Email' endpoint. The exploit should attempt to add the attacker's email address (attacker@gmail.com) to the victim's account.
Step 4: Ensure the CSRF exploit is designed to bypass the missing CSRF token check by leveraging the previously added and removed email address.
Step 5: Send the CSRF exploit payload to the victim (e.g., via a malicious link or embedded in a phishing email).
Step 6: When the victim visits the exploit page, their browser will automatically send the request to add the attacker's email to their account.
Step 7: The attacker will receive the confirmation link on their email address (attacker@gmail.com) for the victim's account.
Step 8: The attacker uses the confirmation link to gain full control of the victim's account.
payloads_or_patterns: <form action='<TARGET_URL>/add_secondary_email' method='POST'> <input type='hidden' name='email' value='attacker@gmail.com'> <!-- Potentially other parameters like '_csrf_token' if it can be left empty or manipulated --> <input type='submit' value='Submit'> </form>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and analyzing requests, and crafting exploit payloads.
Custom Script (Python/JavaScript): To automate the creation and sending of CSRF exploits.
success_indicators: Confirmation email received at the attacker's email address (attacker@gmail.com) for the victim's account.
Successful addition of attacker@gmail.com as a secondary email on the victim's account.
Ability to use the confirmation link to reset the victim's password or access account details.
failure_indicators: Victim's account does not receive the confirmation link.
The application returns a clear error message indicating CSRF token validation failure and does not send the confirmation link.
The secondary email is not added to the victim's account.
Pivot: If CSRF fails due to strict token validation, investigate other vulnerabilities like Open Redirects in conjunction with other parameters or API endpoints.

playbooks.impact: Complete account takeover of the victim's account. This includes accessing sensitive information, performing actions on behalf of the victim, and potentially resetting the password to gain permanent control.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement robust CSRF protection for any state-changing operation, especially those related to account security like adding secondary email addresses. Ensure that previously added but removed email addresses cannot be exploited via CSRF.

meta.title: Exploiting popular macOS apps with a single ".terminal" file.
meta.programs: Internet Bug Bounty, Slack, Keybase, Telegram

target_profile.technology: macOS applications with file sharing functionality
tech_stack_fingerprints: Applications that download and handle files

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a macOS application fails to properly implement or delegate file quarantine to the OS for downloaded files, allowing specially crafted files to bypass Gatekeeper checks.
prerequisites: User must be convinced to download and open a malicious file.
applicability_signals: Application allows direct download and execution of files without applying quarantine attributes.
Application does not implement its own quarantine checks.
Applications that sync files to cloud storage without quarantine attributes.
skip_if: Application correctly applies the 'com.apple.quarantine' extended attribute to all downloaded files.
Application delegates file quarantine to the macOS OS.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify macOS applications that handle file downloads or synchronization.

Step 2: Analyze the file handling mechanism of the target application. Look for evidence that quarantine attributes are not being applied or are being bypassed. This can involve downloading files and inspecting their extended attributes using `xattr -l <filename>`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious payload within a `.terminal` file. This file type does not require executable permissions to run and can execute arbitrary commands.
Step 2: Deliver the `.terminal` file to the victim through the vulnerable application (e.g., as a download, via file sync).
Step 3: The victim downloads and opens the `.terminal` file.
Step 4: If the application failed to quarantine the file, the macOS Gatekeeper will not warn the user, and the payload within the `.terminal` file will execute.
payloads_or_patterns: A `.terminal` file with malicious commands. Example: `echo 'echo "PWNED"' > /tmp/evil.sh; chmod +x /tmp/evil.sh; /tmp/evil.sh`
tools_recommended: macOS Terminal: for crafting and testing `.terminal` files.
xattr command-line tool: for inspecting file extended attributes to verify quarantine status.
success_indicators: Arbitrary commands within the `.terminal` file execute without Gatekeeper warnings.
Observable output from the executed commands (e.g., file creation, network callback, process execution).
failure_indicators: Gatekeeper displays a warning when the `.terminal` file is opened, or the file is blocked. This indicates the application or OS is correctly quarantining files.
The `.terminal` file executes but the payload does not run as expected (e.g., due to incorrect payload syntax or environmental issues).

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary code on the victim's macOS machine by bypassing Gatekeeper, leading to potential data theft, system compromise, or further malicious activity.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that downloaded files are correctly quarantined by the application or the operating system, paying close attention to file types that might have special handling or bypass standard security checks.

meta.title: An unreproducable bug due to the load balancer, an unusual Open Redirect bug

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application incorrectly handles URL redirection, specifically when redirecting from a 'www' subdomain to a non-'www' domain, and fails to properly concatenate the domain and path components.
prerequisites: The target application performs an automatic redirect from www.example.com to example.com.
The redirection logic might involve string manipulation of the URL path.
applicability_signals: Observe the application redirecting www.example.com/some_path to example.com/some_path.
Look for instances where the redirection logic might be concatenating domain and path components without proper delimiters.
skip_if: All redirects are handled correctly, ensuring a '/' character is always present between the domain and path.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks requests containing potentially malicious path segments.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to a known 'www' prefixed URL on the target domain (e.g., www.example.com/any_page) and observe the redirection to the non-'www' version (example.com/any_page).
Step 2: Identify if the redirection logic appears to be concatenating the domain and path without a '/' separator.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL that includes a malicious domain name as if it were part of the path, intended to be concatenated after the base domain. For example, visit www.example.com/<malicious_domain>.
Step 2: Observe the redirection. If the vulnerability exists, the user will be redirected to example.com<malicious_domain> instead of example.com/<malicious_domain>.
Step 3: To confirm, try to trigger the redirect repeatedly or with different injected path segments.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: To repeatedly send the crafted URL and observe the redirection behavior.
success_indicators: The user is redirected to example.com<injected_path> (e.g., example.comevil.com) instead of example.com/<injected_path>.
failure_indicators: The user is consistently redirected to example.com/<injected_path>, indicating the vulnerability is not present or has been fixed. Pivot to testing other redirect mechanisms or vulnerabilities.
The initial request to www.example.com/<malicious_domain> results in an error or unexpected behavior before redirection. This could indicate URL parsing issues or WAF interference.

playbooks.impact: Users can be redirected to arbitrary external websites controlled by the attacker, leading to phishing attacks, credential theft, or malicious content delivery.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing URL redirection logic, pay close attention to how path components are concatenated with domain names, especially in scenarios involving subdomain changes or canonicalization.

meta.title: How I bypassed 2fa in a 3 years old private program!
meta.category: 2FA / MFA bypass, Bruteforce, Lack of rate limiting

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the 2FA verification endpoint does not properly validate client-side security controls, allowing for brute-force attempts on the Time-based One-Time Password (TOTP).
prerequisites: Attacker must have a valid session token (<SESSION_TOKEN>) for an authenticated user.
The target application must use TOTP for 2FA.
applicability_signals: The /2fa/verify endpoint accepts a JSON payload with a 'code' field.
The /2fa/verify endpoint does not return an 'Invalid Captcha' error when client-side captcha headers are removed.
skip_if: The /2fa/verify endpoint returns an 'Invalid Captcha' error when client-side captcha headers are removed.
The endpoint implements strict rate limiting on verification attempts.
All attempts to guess the TOTP fail with a distinct error message indicating incorrect code, and no 200 OK responses are observed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the 2FA verification endpoint and observe its request/response patterns. Look for headers like 'Authorization' and the structure of the POST body for the verification code.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to /2fa/verify with a valid <SESSION_TOKEN> and a dummy verification code. Observe the response code and message.

Step 2: Remove any client-side captcha-related headers from the request (e.g., 'Google-Captcha-Response'). Send the request again and observe the response. If an 'Invalid Captcha' error is returned, this technique may not work.
Step 3: If no 'Invalid Captcha' error appears, proceed to brute-force the 'code' parameter. Use Burp Suite Intruder or a similar tool to send a large number of requests with varying TOTP codes.
Step 4: Configure the brute-force tool to look for a specific success indicator (e.g., 'Verification successfull' or a 200 OK status code) and a failure indicator (e.g., 'Wrong code! Try again.')
Step 5: Initiate the brute-force attack. Monitor the responses for the success indicator. Given TOTP codes change every ~60 seconds, aim for rapid, high-concurrency requests.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /2fa/verify HTTP/1.1
Host: api.private.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64; rv:77.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/77.0
Accept: application/json, text/plain, */*
Accept-Language: en-GB,en;q=0.5
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Content-Type: application/json;charset=utf-8
Authorization: Bearer <SESSION_TOKEN>
Content-Length: 17
Origin: https://app.private.com
Referer: https://app.private.com

{"code":"<TOTP_CODE>"}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: Configure for high thread count (e.g., 888 threads) and target the 'code' parameter. Use a grep-extract for 'Try again' as a failure indicator.
Turbo Intruder or custom Python/Go script: For potentially faster and more stable brute-force with high concurrency.
success_indicators: HTTP 200 OK response
Response body containing: {"message":"Verification successfull"}
failure_indicators: HTTP 401 Unauthorized response
Response body containing: {"message":"Wrong code! Try again."}
If after many attempts no 200 OK is received, the TOTP might be rotating too fast for the brute-force or a different mechanism is in place.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass multi-factor authentication for any authenticated user, gaining unauthorized access to their account and all associated data or functionalities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test authentication and 2FA mechanisms for brute-force vulnerabilities and lack of proper rate limiting, especially after attempting to bypass client-side security controls.

meta.title: Disclose content of internal Facebook javascript modules (Revisited)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When specific internal Javascript modules are loaded by the Facebook web application and their content is not properly restricted from public access.
applicability_signals: Identification of URLs serving internal Javascript modules that are accessible via unauthenticated requests.
Observation of Javascript files being loaded that contain internal logic or configurations.
skip_if: All accessible Javascript files are minified and obfuscated without revealing meaningful internal details.
Access to any potentially internal Javascript modules is blocked by authentication or authorization checks.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze network traffic on Facebook's web application to identify Javascript files being loaded.
Step 2: Filter for URLs that appear to serve internal modules or scripts, potentially containing keywords like 'internal', 'admin', 'config', or specific module names.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to directly access identified internal Javascript module URLs via unauthenticated HTTP GET requests.
Step 2: If direct access is successful, download and analyze the content of the Javascript files for sensitive information, internal API endpoints, configuration settings, or debugging information.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and analyzing network traffic, and for making direct HTTP requests.
Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting network requests and response bodies.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of Javascript files containing human-readable internal logic, configuration variables, or API endpoints.
Response status code 200 OK for requests to internal Javascript module URLs.
failure_indicators: 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden responses for all identified internal Javascript module URLs, indicating proper access controls.
Javascript files are heavily minified and obfuscated, yielding no discernible internal information.
Successful retrieval of generic or third-party Javascript libraries without specific Facebook internal content.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of internal Facebook Javascript modules can reveal internal logic, configuration settings, API endpoints, debugging information, or other sensitive details about Facebook's infrastructure and operations. This could aid attackers in finding other vulnerabilities or understanding internal workings.
playbooks.key_lesson: Ensure that all internal Javascript modules and their content are properly protected by authentication and authorization mechanisms to prevent unauthorized disclosure.

meta.title: Increasing reward points N number of time

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application awards reward points for quiz completions and there is no check to prevent multiple submissions or plays of the same quiz by the same user.
prerequisites: The user must be authenticated and able to participate in quizzes.
applicability_signals: The existence of an API endpoint that processes quiz answers and awards points.
The absence of a mechanism to track if a quiz has already been played by a user or to limit the number of times points can be awarded.
The application allows earning points from 'free' quizzes.
skip_if: The application implements checks to prevent duplicate quiz submissions or limits the number of times reward points can be earned.
All points awarded are immediately validated against a server-side ledger with strict controls.

recon_steps: Identify the API endpoint used for submitting quiz answers and observe the HTTP request structure (method, parameters, headers).
Analyze the parameters sent in the request to understand how answers, contest IDs, and other relevant data are transmitted.
attack_steps: Intercept the HTTP request after completing a free quiz and earning reward points.
Send this request to Burp Suite Intruder.
Configure Intruder to fuzz the `contestId` or any other parameter that identifies the specific quiz, using a NULL payload.
Set the attack threads to a high number (e.g., 25) to rapidly send multiple requests.
Modify the `NUMBER_OF_TIMES_YOU_WANT_THE_REWARD_POINTS_TO_INCREASE` (conceptually, this is achieved by sending multiple identical requests in quick succession).
Observe the response for each request to see if reward points are successfully incremented.
payloads_or_patterns: Use a NULL payload in Burp Intruder for the relevant parameter (e.g., contestId) and send multiple identical requests to the answer submission endpoint.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Intruder for sending multiple requests rapidly).
success_indicators: The user's reward points balance increases by the expected amount for each successful request, effectively multiplying the earned points.
The server does not return an error or a 'quiz already played' message for subsequent identical submissions.
failure_indicators: The server returns an error like 'Quiz already played', 'Invalid contest ID', or 'You have already earned points for this quiz'. This indicates a server-side check is in place. Pivot to looking for race conditions or other logic flaws if the error is inconsistent.
Reward points do not increase after multiple submissions. This could mean the requests are not being processed correctly or a limit is enforced.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can artificially inflate their reward points balance by repeatedly submitting the same quiz answers, leading to an unfair advantage, potential financial loss for the platform if points are redeemable for monetary value, and disruption of game mechanics.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that the server-side logic prevents duplicate submissions or excessive point accumulation for the same action or resource, especially for actions that award currency or in-game items.

meta.title: Chaining rate limiting for account lockout

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the 'forgot password' functionality exists and appears to lack sufficient rate limiting.
prerequisites: Target application has a 'forgot password' feature.
applicability_signals: Successful interception of the password reset request.
Observation that password reset happens automatically without verification.
skip_if: The 'forgot password' feature requires strict rate limiting (e.g., IP-based rate limits that are easily triggered or multiple verification steps).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the 'forgot password' functionality within the target Android application.
Step 2: Attempt to initiate a password reset for a known user account.
Step 3: Observe the application's response. If a password reset email is not sent and the password is changed automatically, proceed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Intercept the password reset request using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
Step 2: Send the intercepted request to the proxy's intruder tool.
Step 3: Configure the intruder to repeatedly send the password reset request for the target email address at a high frequency (e.g., once per second).
Step 4: Monitor the application or user account to confirm the user is locked out.
payloads_or_patterns: Repeatedly send the POST request to the password reset endpoint with the same email parameter. The exact request depends on the application's implementation.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Intruder functionality for rapid request repetition)
success_indicators: The legitimate user is unable to sign in due to repeated password changes.
The attacker gains access to the account if the password reset process is flawed in a way that allows an attacker to guess or set the password.
failure_indicators: The application imposes strict rate limiting, blocking repeated requests (e.g., 429 Too Many Requests). Pivot: Investigate other unprotected endpoints for rate limiting issues.
The password reset functionality requires additional verification steps (e.g., email confirmation code) that the attacker cannot bypass.

playbooks.impact: Account lockout preventing the legitimate user from accessing their account. This can lead to denial of service and potential user frustration or loss of access to critical services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test the rate limiting on critical authentication and account management functionalities like 'forgot password' by attempting to send requests at a high frequency.

meta.title: How I landed on my first bounty : No SPF / DMARC Record Found leading to Social Engineering Attack

tech_stack_fingerprints: MXToolbox lookup for SPF and DMARC records

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target's domain lacks proper SPF and DMARC DNS records, allowing for impersonation via spoofed emails.
prerequisites: Target domain's DNS records can be queried.
applicability_signals: No SPF record found for the domain.
No DMARC record found for the domain.
skip_if: SPF and DMARC records are correctly configured and enforced.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use an online DNS lookup tool (e.g., MxToolbox, Kitterman SPF Query Tool) to check the SPF record for the target domain.
Step 2: Use an online DNS lookup tool to check the DMARC record for the target domain.
Step 3: Verify if DNS records exist and if they are properly configured to prevent email spoofing.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use a fake mailer service (e.g., emkei.cz) to send a spoofed email that appears to originate from the target domain to an intended recipient.
Step 2: Craft a social engineering message within the spoofed email to trick the recipient into performing a malicious action (e.g., clicking a link, providing credentials).
tools_recommended: MxToolbox for DNS lookups
Kitterman SPF Query Tool for SPF record analysis
emkei.cz for sending spoofed emails
success_indicators: The spoofed email is delivered to the recipient's inbox and is not flagged as spam or suspicious by the email server.
The recipient falls for the social engineering attempt.
failure_indicators: Spoofed emails are consistently rejected or sent to the spam folder.
The recipient recognizes the email as suspicious and does not interact with it.

playbooks.impact: Successful social engineering attacks can lead to account compromise, credential theft, malware distribution, financial fraud, or unauthorized access to sensitive information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for the presence and proper configuration of SPF and DMARC records during reconnaissance, as their absence can enable social engineering attacks through email spoofing.

meta.title: How An API Misconfiguration Can Lead To Your Internal Company Data

affected_components: Algolia API keys with elevated permissions (e.g., 'listIndexes', 'editSettings')
tech_stack_fingerprints: applicationID and APIKEY found in source code or mobile apps

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Algolia API key is found in the source code or mobile applications and has permissions beyond basic search, such as 'listIndexes' or 'editSettings'.
prerequisites: Algolia is used for search functionality on the target website.
Algolia applicationID and API key are exposed or discoverable.
applicability_signals: API key and application ID are present in client-side JavaScript.
API key is found within a mobile application's decompiled code.
The discovered API key has permissions like 'listIndexes' or 'editSettings' when queried.
skip_if: The API key only has 'search' permission.
The API key is a 'Secure Key' (base64 encoded and cannot list permissions).

recon_steps: Step 1: Search the target's website source code (HTML, JavaScript) for 'algolia', 'applicationID', and 'apiKey'.
Step 2: If the target has a mobile application, consider decompiling it to find embedded API credentials.

Step 3: Once applicationID and API key are found, send a GET request to `https://<APPID>-dsn.algolia.net/1/keys/<APIKEY>?x-algolia-application-id=<APPID>&x-algolia-api-key=<APIKEY>` to retrieve the key's permissions.
attack_steps: Step 1: If the API key has 'listIndexes' permission, send a GET request to `https://<application-id>-1.algolianet.com/1/indexes?x-algolia-application-id=<application-id>&x-algolia-api-key=<example-key>` to enumerate all available indexes.
Step 2: If 'editSettings' permission is present, craft a PUT request to modify index settings, e.g., to inject JavaScript.

Step 3: For 'editSettings' XSS, send a PUT request to `https://<application-id>-1.algolianet.com/1/indexes/<index-name>/settings` with a JSON payload containing `<script>alert(1);</script>` in fields like `highlightPreTag`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP for intercepting and modifying requests.
Curl for direct API requests.
Mobile app decompilation tools (e.g., jadx for Android, Hopper Disassembler for iOS).
success_indicators: Response to GET `/keys/<APIKEY>` shows permissions like 'listIndexes' or 'editSettings'.
Response to GET `/indexes` lists sensitive index names containing internal company data.
XSS payload `alert(1)` executes in the browser when searching a page whose Algolia index settings were modified.
failure_indicators: The API key only has 'search' permission and no other sensitive permissions are found.
No Algolia credentials are found in client-side code or mobile apps.
Requests to modify settings are rejected with a 4xx or 5xx error, indicating insufficient permissions or incorrect endpoint usage.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive internal company documents (security policies, business operations, Slack messages) via 'listIndexes' permission. Potential for cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks, credential theft, or sensitive data exfiltration via 'editSettings' permission by injecting malicious JavaScript into search results.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize API keys found in client-side code or mobile applications for excessive permissions beyond their intended use, especially on services like Algolia where 'listIndexes' and 'editSettings' can lead to significant data disclosure and code execution vulnerabilities.

meta.title: See whether a Hackercup Facebook participant allows recruitment contact

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user's profile on the Facebook Coding Competitions portal is publicly accessible and the 'Private Contact Info' section is displayed.
prerequisites: The target user must be a participant in a Facebook Hackercup competition.
applicability_signals: The presence of a 'Private Contact Info' section on a participant's profile.
The 'recruitment_preference' field being present in the user's data payload.
skip_if: The 'recruitment_preference' field is not present in the user's profile data.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to a known Facebook Hackercup participant's profile page at <TARGET_URL>/codingcompetitions/profile to observe the profile structure and available information.
Step 2: Inspect the network requests made by the profile page to identify the API endpoint and data structure used to retrieve participant information.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain the user ID of a Hackercup participant.
Step 2: Make a request to the relevant API endpoint that retrieves participant data, including the user ID.
Step 3: Analyze the JSON response to check for the presence and value of the 'recruitment_preference' field.
payloads_or_patterns: insufficient detail in writeup - API request structure and parameters are not explicitly detailed, but the response structure is provided: {"data":{"node":{"__typename":"CodingContestIndividualEntrant","entrant_personal_info":{"recruitment_preference":"ALLOW_RECRUITMENT_CONTACT","individual_entrant_user":{"id":"<USER_ID>"}}}},"extensions":{"is_final":true}}
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools (Network Tab) to inspect API requests and responses.
Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP to intercept and analyze HTTP traffic.
success_indicators: The JSON response contains the field 'recruitment_preference' with a value of 'ALLOW_RECRUITMENT_CONTACT' or 'DISALLOW_RECRUITMENT_CONTACT'.
failure_indicators: The 'recruitment_preference' field is missing from the response, or the API endpoint is inaccessible for the given user ID. In such cases, investigate other Hackercup participant IDs or explore different competition years.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can determine if any Facebook Hackercup participant is open to recruitment, allowing for targeted outreach by recruiters. This could be used to enumerate all entrants of a competition and their recruitment preferences.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing user profiles, always examine all displayed fields and their underlying data payloads for potential privacy or information disclosure vulnerabilities, even if they seem minor.

meta.title: Exploiting Application Logic to Referral Code Disclosure

tech_stack_fingerprints: Response containing JSON with referralCode key

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access their profile information via an API endpoint, and the referral code functionality is documented as requiring a prior order.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session with the target application.
applicability_signals: API endpoint exists for retrieving customer profile data.
The UI indicates that referral codes are only available after the first order.
skip_if: API endpoint returns 401 Unauthorized without valid session tokens.
API endpoint does not return a 'referralCode' field in the response.

recon_steps: Intercept and analyze the HTTP request made when updating profile information.
Identify the endpoint used for profile updates (e.g., PUT /v1/customer/<UUID>/profile).
Observe the request headers and body for profile update requests.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to the identified profile endpoint (e.g., GET /v1/customer/<UUID>/profile) with the attacker's session cookies.
Step 2: Remove any request body that was present in the original PUT request.
Step 3: Analyze the JSON response for the presence of a 'referralCode' field.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use Repeater to modify the HTTP request method from PUT to GET and remove the request body.
success_indicators: The API response contains a JSON object with a 'referralCode' key and a corresponding value.
The returned referral code is valid and can be used by the attacker or others.
failure_indicators: The API returns a 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden error.
The API response does not contain the 'referralCode' field.
The API requires additional parameters in the GET request that were not obvious from the PUT request.

playbooks.impact: Allows any authenticated user to obtain a referral code without fulfilling the prerequisite of placing an order, potentially bypassing business logic designed to incentivize first-time purchases and leading to misuse of referral programs.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test API endpoints that update or retrieve user profile data by changing the HTTP method to GET and removing the request body, as application logic flaws may permit access to sensitive information or features not intended for immediate disclosure.

meta.title: Global grant uri in Android 8.0-9.0

tech_stack_fingerprints: Android application code demonstrating usage of Content Providers and Intents

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Android application can trigger another application's Content Provider with specific Intent flags, particularly on Android versions 8.0-9.0.
prerequisites: The attacker controls a third-party Android application.
The target Android OS version is between 8.0 and 9.0 (inclusive).
applicability_signals: Presence of `Intent.FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION`, `Intent.FLAG_GRANT_WRITE_URI_PERMISSION`, or `Intent.FLAG_GRANT_PERSISTABLE_URI_PERMISSION` flags in app code.
Usage of `content://` URIs to access data from other applications.
skip_if: The target Android OS version is outside the 8.0-9.0 range.
Content Providers are not exposed or accessible via intents.
